title: Milestone 3: Hardware Validation
description: Validate against real target hardware on Linux. Capture fresh serial transcripts, update fixtures, document known failure modes, and run portability checks on macOS/Windows. Exit criteria: successful repeated recoveries on Linux; macOS/Windows remain portability targets.

title: First real-device recovery on Linux
description: Run unbrk recover against the actual Nokia Valyrian board connected via FTDI FT232R on Linux. This is the critical validation step - everything before this was tested against simulated fixtures only. Test both recovery-only (stop at U-Boot) and full flash-persistent flow. Capture timing data, verify prompt patterns match real board output, note any discrepancies from the documented protocol. Multiple repeated recoveries are required to build confidence. The plan says: 'successful repeated recoveries on target hardware from Linux' is the M3 exit criterion. Document exact steps, board revision, firmware version, adapter details.

comments.author: ynezz
comments.text: This is the moment of truth. All design decisions made during M0-M2 get validated against real hardware. Key things to verify: (1) prompt patterns match real board output exactly, (2) XMODEM timing is compatible with the board's receiver, (3) U-Boot command parsing handles real console output including ANSI escape sequences, (4) the EOT quirk tolerance works as designed, (5) flash write verification catches actual mismatches. If anything doesn't work, the fix path goes back through M1 core tasks. Document everything - board revision, adapter model, USB hub chain, OS kernel version, exact commands run.

title: Capture fresh serial transcripts from real hardware
description: During real-device validation, capture multiple fresh serial transcripts using --transcript-file. These transcripts serve two purposes: (1) update/validate existing fixture data, (2) check for bootloader revision differences that might affect prompt patterns. The plan warns: 'A single captured recovery can bias matcher design toward one bootloader revision or timing profile.' Capture at least 3-5 complete recovery sessions to understand timing variation. Include both clean runs and intentionally interrupted runs. Compare against docs/valyrian-end-to-end.log for consistency.

title: Update fixtures from real transcripts
description: Process the fresh serial transcripts captured from real hardware and update tests/fixtures/valyrian/ accordingly. Add new fixture scenarios that cover any timing or formatting variations observed in real sessions. Verify that all existing tests still pass against the updated fixtures. If real transcripts reveal prompt text or timing that differs from the initial end-to-end log, update the target profile and prompt matchers accordingly. The plan says: 'Re-capture transcripts from additional recovery attempts and bootloader revisions before declaring prompt heuristics stable.'

title: Document known failure modes and recovery guidance
description: Document all failure modes observed during hardware validation: what went wrong, how it manifested in the console output, what the operator should do. Include: timeout scenarios (board not in recovery mode, cable issues), unexpected prompts (different bootloader version), XMODEM failures (timing issues, corrupted transfers), flash verification mismatches. Update docs/testing.md with hardware test procedures. Update README with operator guidance. The plan says: 'known failure modes documented' is part of the M3 exit criteria.

title: macOS portability validation
description: Verify unbrk builds and passes all unit/integration tests on macOS. Run the full CI suite locally on macOS. If a Mac with a serial adapter and the target board is available, attempt a real recovery. Otherwise, document macOS as a 'portability target' that compiles and passes simulated tests but has not been hardware-validated. The plan says: 'macOS and Windows remain portability targets unless and until they each have their own successful real-device recovery evidence.'

title: Windows portability validation
description: Verify unbrk builds and passes unit tests on Windows. Run transport-agnostic integration tests. The serialport crate supports Windows COM ports. If a Windows machine with serial adapter is available, attempt real recovery. Otherwise, document as portability target only. The plan says Windows should have 'compile, unit tests, and any transport-agnostic integration tests to prove portability.'

title: Milestone 2: Usable CLI
description: Build the CLI binary with clap argument parsing, TTY-aware progress UI, JSON event mode, explicit flash/resume modes, console handoff, and utility commands (ports, doctor, completions, man). Exit criteria: human e2e flow is usable without reading source; agent mode is deterministic and documented.

comments.author: ynezz
comments.text: The CLI is a thin rendering layer over the core library. All protocol intelligence lives in unbrk-core; the CLI just parses args, selects output mode, and renders events. The --json mode is the machine interface contract - once published, its event schema and exit codes become API. The --flash-persistent flag is the safety gate for destructive operations. The --non-interactive + --json combination is the agent automation path. The interactive console handoff is the debugging escape hatch.

title: CLI argument parsing + TTY detection + exit codes
description: Implement the full CLI entry point: clap (derive API) argument parsing, TTY detection, and exit code mapping. Commands: recover, ports, completions, man, doctor. Recover flags: --port, --baud, --preloader, --fip, --prompt-timeout, --packet-timeout, --xmodem-retry, --command-timeout, --reset-timeout, --log-file, --transcript-file, --uboot-prompt, --flash-persistent, --resume-from-uboot, --progress auto|plain|fancy|off, --non-interactive, --json, --no-console, plus expert block-layout overrides. TTY detection: use is-terminal crate. Auto-select fancy progress for TTY, plain for non-TTY, off for --json. Validation: --json implies --no-console, --non-interactive requires explicit --port, --progress and --json are mutually exclusive. Exit codes: 0=success, 1=serial error, 2=timeout, 3=protocol error, 4=XMODEM failure, 5=U-Boot command failure, 6=verification mismatch, 7=bad input, 8=user abort.

comments.author: ynezz
comments.text: Flag interaction rules to enforce in clap validation: (1) --json implies --no-console, reject if both --json and explicit --no-console=false, (2) --non-interactive requires explicit --port (no heuristic port selection in automation paths that can erase flash), (3) --flash-persistent is required before any flash operations, (4) --resume-from-uboot is expert-only (consider a warning), (5) --progress is human-only, --json is machine-only, reject if both set. These rules prevent dangerous misconfigurations in automated pipelines.

comments.author: ynezz
comments.text: REVISED: Removed dependency on core state machine (unbrk-g5s.8). CLI argument definitions come from the plan document, not from core code. This unblocks M2 CLI work to proceed in parallel with M1 core work - the clap struct can be fully defined and tested before the state machine exists. The actual wiring of CLI args to core functions happens in unbrk-akl.7 (recovery orchestration modes).

title: doctor command (environment diagnostics)
description: Implement 'unbrk doctor' that checks the environment is ready for recovery: serial port accessible, images readable, sizes fit flash allocations, OS/version info. DEFERRED: The recover command itself should give clear errors for missing ports/files. Doctor is a nice-to-have diagnostic that makes more sense after hardware validation establishes what operators commonly get wrong.

title: Shell completions + manpage generation
description: Implement 'unbrk completions <shell>' (bash, zsh, fish, powershell, elvish) using clap_complete, and 'unbrk man' using clap_mangen. Also wire both into xtask for build-time generation for packaging. These are small tasks (~10 lines each) that share the same dependency on clap and xtask.

title: man command (manpage generation)
description: Implement 'unbrk man' that generates or displays a manpage. Use clap_mangen crate. Wire into xtask for build-time generation. The manpage should document all commands, flags, exit codes, and the recovery workflow.
close_reason: Merged into unbrk-akl.11 (completions + man are both ~10 lines, same deps)

title: TTY detection and output mode selection
description: Implement TTY detection and automatic output mode selection. Rules from the plan: (1) use fancy progress only when stdout is a TTY, (2) disable ANSI and spinners for non-TTY or --json, (3) auto-select port only in interactive mode with exactly one plausible candidate, (4) --non-interactive forbids ambiguous prompts and heuristic port selection. Use atty or is-terminal crate for detection. The --progress flag overrides auto-detection: auto (default, TTY-aware), plain (no ANSI), fancy (force ANSI), off (no progress). This determines the rendering strategy for the entire session.
close_reason: Merged into unbrk-akl.1 (TTY detection is ~10 lines, part of CLI setup)

title: indicatif progress rendering (phase-based)
description: Implement TTY-aware progress rendering using indicatif. Phase-based display from the plan: 'Waiting for recovery mode -> Sending x for preloader -> Uploading preloader -> Waiting for stage 2 prompt -> Sending x for FIP -> Uploading FIP -> Waiting for live U-Boot prompt', followed by flash phases when --flash-persistent is set. Features: spinner for waiting states, progress bar with bytes/throughput/elapsed for XMODEM transfers, clear status banners for transitions, compact recovery summary at end, helpful remediation hints on timeout. Subscribe to the structured event stream from core and render each event type appropriately.

title: JSON event output (NDJSON, schema versioned)
description: Implement --json mode that emits newline-delimited JSON events to stdout. Requirements from the plan: (1) stream declares its schema version in the opening event, (2) --json implies --no-console (enforced by clap validation), (3) events are the same structured types from core, serialized via serde_json, (4) stable event kinds from the plan's list (port_opened, prompt_seen, etc.), (5) each event has a timestamp, kind, and kind-specific payload. This is the machine-readable interface for agents, scripts, and CI. The schema version enables agents to reject incompatible output versions.

title: Exit code mapping for documented failure classes
description: Map core error taxonomy variants to stable exit codes. Proposed mapping: 0=success, 1=serial open failure, 2=timeout, 3=prompt mismatch, 4=XMODEM failure, 5=U-Boot command failure, 6=verification mismatch, 7=bad input, 8=user abort, 9=image too large for flash. These exit codes are part of the machine interface contract. Document them in --help and in README. The plan says a sender-library XMODEM failure should only become terminal if the expected next prompt never appears (see EOT quirk tolerance).
close_reason: Merged into unbrk-akl.1 (exit code mapping is a simple match, part of CLI setup)

title: Console handoff (interactive terminal after recovery)
description: After recovery reaches the RAM-resident U-Boot prompt (and --flash-persistent is NOT set), hand off the serial port to the user as an interactive terminal session. Conditions: only when stdout is a TTY, --json is not active, --no-console is not active. The plan says: 'only offer interactive console handoff when stdout is a TTY and neither --json nor --no-console is active.' Implementation: put terminal in raw mode, bridge stdin<->serial bidirectionally, handle Ctrl-C/Ctrl-D for clean exit. This lets operators manually interact with the RAM-resident U-Boot for debugging before deciding whether to flash.

title: Recovery orchestration modes (flash-persistent + resume-from-uboot)
description: Wire the core recovery and flash logic into the CLI recover command with two explicit modes: (1) --flash-persistent: after reaching U-Boot prompt, invoke the flash plan executor with images from --preloader and --fip. Default behavior WITHOUT this flag stops at the RAM-resident U-Boot prompt. This flag is the safety gate for destructive flash operations. (2) --resume-from-uboot: skip BootROM/XMODEM recovery phase, resume at an existing interactive U-Boot prompt (send CR, wait for prompt), then either hand off to console or enter flash plan if --flash-persistent is also set. Expert-only mode. Report whether the run stopped at temporary U-Boot or completed the persistent flash-and-reset sequence.

title: resume-from-uboot mode
description: Implement --resume-from-uboot which skips the BootROM/XMODEM recovery phase and resumes at an existing interactive U-Boot prompt. Expert-only mode for when the board is already sitting at AN7581>. Then either: (1) hand off to interactive console (default), or (2) enter flash plan if --flash-persistent is also set. The plan says: 'This mode should be treated as expert-only because the operator has to know the current U-Boot context is safe for the requested next step.' Implementation: skip state machine, go directly to ensure_uboot_prompt (send CR, wait for prompt), then branch on --flash-persistent.
close_reason: Merged into unbrk-akl.7 (both are wiring CLI flags to core orchestration)

title: Port auto-detection and ports command
description: Implement 'unbrk ports' to list available serial ports with device info. Also implement auto-detection for 'recover' in interactive mode: filter obviously irrelevant devices, auto-select only when exactly one plausible candidate remains. In non-interactive mode, require explicit --port. The plan says: 'Auto-select a port only in interactive mode, and only when exactly one plausible candidate remains after filtering obviously irrelevant devices.' Use serialport's available_ports() enumeration. Filter heuristics: look for USB serial adapters (FTDI, CP210x, CH340), exclude Bluetooth serial, exclude modem devices.

title: Milestone 1: Recovery Core
description: Implement the full recovery core library: serial transport abstraction, prompt-driven state machine, XMODEM send, U-Boot command runner, Valyrian flash-plan executor, transcript logging, and transcript-backed test fixtures. Exit criteria: happy-path e2e recovery works against simulated fixtures with clear failures for timeout/mismatch/command-failure scenarios.

comments.author: ynezz
comments.text: This is the largest and most critical milestone. The recovery core must be fully testable without hardware via the transport trait abstraction. The state machine design is constrained by the Valyrian protocol's quirks: (1) stage 2 prompt contains stage 1 text, requiring state-scoped matching, (2) the board can advance before XMODEM EOT ACK, requiring forward-progress tolerance, (3) filesize is hex without 0x prefix. All of these are documented in docs/valyrian-uart-recovery-protocol.md and demonstrated in tools/valyrian_uart_recovery.py. The Python helper is the reference implementation - replicate its behavior in Rust, then improve on it (image size validation, structured events, proper error taxonomy).

title: Serial transport trait abstraction
description: Define the core transport trait that abstracts serial port I/O. This trait must support: read(buf) -> Result<usize>, write(data) -> Result<()>, flush() -> Result<()>, and set_timeout(duration). The trait is the seam that separates protocol logic from platform-specific serial I/O, enabling: (1) unit testing with mock transports, (2) integration testing with PTY-backed fakes, (3) real hardware with serialport crate. Design considerations from the protocol doc: XMODEM readiness detection needs raw byte access (not decoded text), so the trait must operate on &[u8] not strings. The trait should NOT assume any particular framing or flow control - those are configuration details for the concrete implementation.

comments.author: ynezz
comments.text: Design constraint: the trait must support both synchronous (blocking reads with timeout) and the XMODEM sender's needs (getc/putc style callbacks). The Python helper uses a single serial.Serial object for both prompt reading and XMODEM transfer, switching the timeout dynamically. In Rust, we can either: (a) make the trait support timeout reconfiguration, or (b) have the XMODEM sender take ownership temporarily. Option (a) is simpler and matches the Python pattern. The trait should NOT be async - UART I/O is inherently synchronous at 115200 baud, and adding async would add complexity without benefit for this use case.

title: XMODEM-CRC sender with unit tests
description: Implement XMODEM-CRC sender (128-byte blocks) and its tests. Protocol: SOH(0x01) + seq(u8) + ~seq(u8) + data(128 bytes, padded) + CRC-H + CRC-L. Wait for ACK(0x06) or NAK(0x15). Send EOT(0x04) at end. CRC-16/XMODEM polynomial. Consider implementing from scratch (~200-300 lines) rather than depending on a crate, for full control over the EOT handling and progress callbacks. Tests must cover: successful transfer, CRC correctness, NAK retransmission, EOT handshake (ACK and NAK), timeout, progress callback, small file (padding), exact block boundary, file one byte over boundary.

comments.author: ynezz
comments.text: Key implementation decision: use an existing XMODEM crate or implement from scratch? The xmodem crate on crates.io exists but: (1) may not handle the Valyrian EOT quirk gracefully, (2) may not provide the callback hooks needed for progress reporting, (3) adds a dependency we'd need to audit. A hand-rolled sender for XMODEM-CRC (128-byte blocks) is ~200-300 lines of Rust and gives us full control. The protocol is simple: SOH(0x01) + seq(u8) + ~seq(u8) + data(128 bytes) + CRC-H(u8) + CRC-L(u8). Wait for ACK(0x06) or NAK(0x15). Send EOT(0x04) at end. CRC-16/XMODEM polynomial: x^16 + x^12 + x^5 + 1.

title: XMODEM readiness detection (raw byte CRC 'C')
description: Detect XMODEM-CRC readiness by watching for repeated 'C' (0x43) bytes in the raw serial stream. The protocol doc says: 'XMODEM readiness detection should inspect raw bytes, not only decoded text, because the observed UART stream mixes control bytes with the repeated C characters.' The Python helper looks for b'CCC' in the raw buffer. Implementation must: (1) scan raw bytes, not UTF-8 decoded text, (2) be tolerant of non-printable control bytes mixed between C characters, (3) have a configurable timeout for the readiness wait, (4) advance the buffer cursor past the readiness marker so subsequent prompt matching doesn't re-match stale C bytes. This is a separate concern from the XMODEM sender - readiness detection happens before the sender starts.

title: EOT quirk tolerance (forward progress on early board advance)
description: Handle the observed Valyrian quirk where the board can advance to the next prompt BEFORE acknowledging the final XMODEM EOT. The plan says: 'If the console advances to the next prompt or to U-Boot before the sender sees a clean final EOT acknowledgement, treat that as forward progress with a warning instead of an unconditional transfer failure.' This means the XMODEM sender's return value of 'transfer failed' should not be terminal if the state machine observes the expected next prompt in the console output. Implementation: after XMODEM send returns (success or failure), check for the next expected prompt. If found, log a warning but continue. If neither EOT-ACK nor next prompt arrives within timeout, THEN fail. The Python helper does exactly this pattern - see preloader_result.ok check followed by wait_for_text for the second prompt.
close_reason: Merged into unbrk-g5s.8 (EOT quirk tolerance is a conditional in the state machine, not separate code)

comments.author: ynezz
comments.text: This quirk is well-documented in the existing notes and demonstrated in the Python helper. The observed behavior: after the host sends the final XMODEM block and EOT, the board sometimes responds with the next prompt (Press x to load BL31...) BEFORE sending the XMODEM ACK for EOT. The Python helper handles this: 'if not result.ok: info("...checking for the next prompt because this target can jump ahead...")'. Our implementation should: (1) attempt EOT handshake, (2) if it times out, check if the next expected prompt already appeared, (3) if yes, log warning and continue, (4) if neither, fail with clear error.

title: U-Boot command executor + output parsing
description: Implement the complete U-Boot interaction layer: (1) Command executor: send command string over serial, wait for U-Boot prompt to reappear, return console output. Based on Python helper's run_uboot_command(). (2) Environment parser: parse 'printenv loadaddr' and 'printenv filesize' output. Critical Valyrian quirk: filesize is hex WITHOUT 0x prefix (e.g. 'filesize=1a2b3c'). Parse both with-prefix and without-prefix cases. Return strongly-typed values (FileSize, LoadAddr). (3) Command success verification: parse 'blocks erased: OK', 'blocks written: OK', 'Total Size = 0xNNNN = NNNN Bytes' from console output. Return typed result, not just bool. Each command has configurable timeout (default 30s). Include unit tests for: loadaddr parsing, filesize with/without 0x prefix, success/failure pattern matching, malformed output.

title: U-Boot environment parser (loadaddr, filesize)
description: Parse U-Boot environment variable output from 'printenv' commands. Key variables: loadaddr (memory address for loadx/mmc write) and filesize (size of last transferred file). Critical Valyrian quirk: filesize is emitted as hexadecimal WITHOUT a '0x' prefix (e.g. 'filesize=1a2b3c' not 'filesize=0x1a2b3c'). The Python helper handles this in parse_u_boot_hex() which tries '0x' prefix first, then falls back to base-16. The plan says: 'parse filesize as hexadecimal even when U-Boot omits the 0x prefix.' Parse 'filesize=<value>' and 'loadaddr=<value>' patterns from printenv output. Return strongly-typed values (e.g. FileSize(usize), LoadAddr(u64)) rather than raw strings.
close_reason: Merged into unbrk-g5s.13 (U-Boot executor now includes env parsing)

comments.author: ynezz
comments.text: The filesize parsing quirk is specific to the AN7581 board's U-Boot build. The printenv output shows 'filesize=1a2b3c' (no 0x prefix). The Python helper's parse_u_boot_hex() tries int(value, 0) first (auto-detect base), which fails on bare hex, then falls back to int(value, 16). In Rust, we should: (1) check for 0x/0X prefix -> parse as hex, (2) otherwise try hex first, (3) this is board-specific behavior that should be documented in the target profile. The loadaddr variable typically DOES have the 0x prefix (loadaddr=0x46000000), so both cases must be handled.

title: Console success verification for MMC commands
description: Verify U-Boot MMC command success by parsing console output patterns. The plan requires: 'command success must come from parsing the console output (blocks erased: OK, blocks written: OK, Total Size, filesize=), not from assuming that command echo implies success.' Must detect: (1) 'blocks erased: OK' after 'mmc erase', (2) 'blocks written: OK' after 'mmc write', (3) 'Total Size = 0xNNNN = NNNN Bytes' after loadx, (4) failure indicators. The Python helper uses require_output() with regex patterns to enforce these checks. Return a typed result that distinguishes success/failure with the parsed values, not just bool.
close_reason: Merged into unbrk-g5s.13 (U-Boot executor now includes success verification)

title: Flash plan types + image size validation
description: Define flash plan data types and implement pre-flash safety validation. Types: EraseRange { start_block: BlockOffset, block_count: BlockCount }, WriteStage { start_block, block_count, image_path }, FlashPlan { erase_ranges, write_stages }. Use strong types throughout. Image size validation: before any erase/write, confirm each image fits within its allocated MMC block range (max_bytes = block_count * block_size). Valyrian limits: preloader max 0xfc*512=32256 bytes, FIP max 0x700*512=917504 bytes. If exceeded, abort with clear error BEFORE touching flash. This is a SAFETY-CRITICAL check the Python helper does NOT do - the plan explicitly requires it. Include unit tests: image exactly fits, smaller, exceeds by 1 byte, exceeds by full block, zero-size image.

title: Valyrian flash plan executor + reset evidence detection
description: Implement the end-to-end flash plan executor for the Valyrian board, including post-reset observation. Full sequence: (1) ensure U-Boot is interactive (send CR, wait for prompt), (2) read loadaddr via printenv, (3) validate image sizes against block counts BEFORE erasing, (4) mmc erase 0 0x800, verify 'blocks erased: OK', (5) loadx $loadaddr 115200 for preloader, wait for CRC readiness, send via XMODEM, verify filesize, (6) mmc write $loadaddr 0x4 0xfc, verify success, (7) repeat for FIP at 0x100/0x700, (8) send 'reset', (9) detect reset evidence ('EcoNet System Reset' or similar). The plan distinguishes three levels: 'flash sequence completed', 'reset observed', 'persistent boot verified'. Only report what was observed - do NOT claim persistent boot is verified. This is the most complex single piece - it orchestrates U-Boot commands, XMODEM, env parsing, and success verification.

comments.author: ynezz
comments.text: This is the most complex single task. It orchestrates 6+ sequential U-Boot commands over serial, with XMODEM transfers embedded in the sequence. The Python helper's flash_from_uboot() function is the reference. Key improvement over the Python helper: validate image sizes BEFORE erasing flash (the Python helper does not do this). The sequence is fail-fast: any command failure should abort before the next command runs. Never leave the board in a half-written state if we can detect the failure early enough.

title: Image size validation (fail before flash if too large)
description: Validate that each image file fits within its allocated MMC block range BEFORE issuing any erase or write commands. The plan says: 'confirm that each configured flash range is large enough for its host image using the profile's MMC block size, and fail closed if either image would overrun its allotted block count.' Calculation: max_bytes = block_count * block_size_bytes. For Valyrian: preloader max = 0xfc * 512 = 32256 bytes, FIP max = 0x700 * 512 = 917504 bytes. If the image file exceeds max_bytes, abort with a clear error BEFORE touching flash. This is a safety-critical check - writing an oversized image would corrupt adjacent flash regions. The Python helper does NOT do this check, but the plan explicitly requires it as an improvement over the helper.
close_reason: Merged into unbrk-g5s.16 (validation is a method on flash plan types)

comments.author: ynezz
comments.text: SAFETY-CRITICAL: This validation must run BEFORE any mmc erase command. An oversized image written to flash would corrupt data beyond the allocated region, potentially bricking the board in a way that even BootROM recovery can't fix (depending on what's overwritten). The Python helper does NOT do this check - it's an explicit improvement called out in the plan. Calculation: image_size_bytes <= block_count * 512 (MMC block size). Fail with a clear error message showing actual vs max size.

title: Reset evidence detection
description: After sending 'reset' to U-Boot, detect evidence that the reset command executed. The plan distinguishes three levels: (1) 'flash sequence completed' - all writes succeeded, (2) 'reset observed' - console shows 'resetting ...' or 'EcoNet System Reset', (3) 'persistent boot verified' - the board boots from rewritten flash (NOT achievable from UART alone). The Python helper waits for 'EcoNet System Reset|Press x|U-Boot' after reset, with a 20s timeout. We should: detect reset evidence, report it, but NOT claim the persistent boot is verified. The plan says: 'do not treat a bare Press x prompt or generic U-Boot banner as proof that the newly written persistent boot path is healthy.' Report what was observed and let the operator assess.
close_reason: Merged into unbrk-g5s.17 (reset detection is the final step of the flash executor)

title: Real serial backend (serialport crate)
description: Implement the serial transport trait using the serialport crate for real hardware. Configuration: 115200 baud, 8N1, no flow control (matching Valyrian defaults). The serialport crate is the de facto cross-platform serial library for Rust, supporting Linux, macOS, and Windows. Must handle: port opening with clear error messages (permission denied, port busy, port not found), timeout configuration, and graceful close. On Linux the FTDI FT232R adapter presents as /dev/ttyUSB0. Consider: should we depend on serialport directly or via a thin wrapper? Direct is simpler given we forbid unsafe and serialport handles the platform FFI.

title: Structured event types for recovery progress
description: Define the structured event enum shared by human and agent output modes. Events from the plan: port_opened, prompt_seen, input_sent, crc_ready, xmodem_started, xmodem_progress, xmodem_completed, uboot_prompt_seen, uboot_command_started, uboot_command_completed, image_verified, reset_seen, handoff_ready, failure. Each event should carry: timestamp, event kind, and kind-specific payload. The event stream should declare its schema version in the opening event so agents can reject incompatible output. Events must be serializable to JSON (serde) for --json mode and to human-readable strings for TTY mode. The event model is the contract between core and CLI - core emits events, CLI renders them.

comments.author: ynezz
comments.text: The event model is the contract between core and CLI. Events must be: (1) serializable to JSON (serde), (2) renderable to human-friendly strings, (3) versioned from the first release. The opening event should include: schema_version, tool_version, target_profile, serial_port, timestamp. Consider using a monotonically increasing event sequence number for ordering. The plan lists 14 event kinds - keep this list stable once published, as agents will pattern-match on event kinds.

title: Error taxonomy with stable exit codes
description: Define the error enum that covers all recovery failure modes, mapped to stable exit codes. From the plan: serial_open_failure, timeout, prompt_mismatch, xmodem_failure, uboot_command_failure, verification_mismatch, bad_input, user_abort. Each variant should carry context (which state, what was expected vs observed, last N bytes of console). Use thiserror for the library error type. The CLI maps these to documented exit codes (e.g. 1=serial error, 2=timeout, 3=protocol error, 4=verification error, etc.). The plan says: 'Prefer explicit types and stable error enums over ad hoc strings.' These exit codes become part of the machine interface contract once published.

title: Convert docs/valyrian-end-to-end.log into test fixtures
description: Parse the existing end-to-end log (docs/valyrian-end-to-end.log) and extract fixture data for transcript-backed tests. Create tests/fixtures/valyrian/ directory with scenario-named fixture files. Extract: (1) initial recovery prompt text, (2) XMODEM CRC readiness byte sequence, (3) preloader transfer bytes, (4) inter-stage boot chatter, (5) second prompt text, (6) FIP transfer bytes, (7) U-Boot boot noise including ANSI escape sequences, (8) AN7581> prompt. The plan emphasizes: 'Convert the existing end-to-end log into fixtures immediately, then add multiple fresh captures.' This log is our only ground truth until we run on real hardware again. Keep fixtures small and named by scenario (happy-path.bin, boot-noise.bin, etc.).

comments.author: ynezz
comments.text: The existing end-to-end log (docs/valyrian-end-to-end.log) is our only protocol ground truth until fresh hardware captures are available. Fixture extraction should preserve: (1) exact byte sequences including control characters and ANSI escapes, (2) the timing relationship between prompts (which bytes came before/after each transition), (3) XMODEM protocol bytes (SOH, ACK, NAK, CRC bytes, EOT). Name fixtures by scenario: happy-path-stage1-prompt.bin, happy-path-crc-readiness.bin, boot-noise-before-uboot.bin, etc. These become the regression test baseline.

title: Fixture test harness (transcript replay)
description: Build a test harness that loads fixture files and replays them through the mock transport to drive the state machine. The harness should: (1) load a fixture scenario, (2) configure a MockTransport with the fixture's byte sequences, (3) run the state machine against it, (4) assert expected state transitions and events, (5) support injecting failures at specific points for error-path testing. This is the bridge between raw fixture data and executable tests. It should be reusable across all test scenarios (happy path, timeouts, mismatches, partial reads). Consider making it a test utility module in unbrk-core that's only compiled under #[cfg(test)].

title: Unit tests: prompt parsing and state transitions
description: Write unit tests for prompt parsing and state machine transitions. Must cover: (1) first prompt detected correctly, (2) second prompt detected correctly and not confused with first, (3) prompt split across multiple reads (fragmentation), (4) prompt with mixed line endings (CR, LF, CRLF), (5) prompt with surrounding noise, (6) timeout when prompt never arrives, (7) state transitions emit correct events, (8) cursor advances correctly so stale text can't re-match. These tests validate the most critical correctness property of the system - getting prompt matching wrong means the recovery either stalls or sends XMODEM data at the wrong time.
close_reason: Merged into unbrk-g5s.7 (unit tests are part of implementation)

title: Unit tests: XMODEM send with mocked transport
description: Write unit tests for the XMODEM sender against mocked transports. Must cover: (1) successful 128-byte block transfer, (2) CRC calculation correctness, (3) NAK retransmission, (4) EOT handshake (both ACK and NAK responses), (5) timeout on receiver silence, (6) progress callback invocation, (7) small file (< 128 bytes, requires padding), (8) exact multiple of 128 bytes, (9) file that's one byte over a block boundary. Use the mock transport to script receiver responses (ACK, NAK, CRC 'C' bytes). Verify correct framing: SOH + seq + ~seq + data + CRC-H + CRC-L.
close_reason: Merged into unbrk-g5s.10 (unit tests are part of implementation)

title: Unit tests: U-Boot environment and command parsing
description: Write unit tests for U-Boot output parsing. Must cover: (1) 'loadaddr=0x46000000' parsing, (2) 'filesize=1a2b3c' parsing (hex WITHOUT 0x prefix), (3) 'filesize=0x1a2b3c' parsing (hex WITH prefix), (4) 'blocks erased: OK' detection, (5) 'blocks written: OK' detection, (6) 'Total Size = 0xNNNN = NNNN Bytes' parsing, (7) filesize mismatch detection, (8) missing variable output, (9) malformed output. The hex-without-prefix case is a known Valyrian quirk that must be covered explicitly.
close_reason: Merged into unbrk-g5s.13 (tests are part of implementation)

title: Unit tests: flash plan validation (image size checks)
description: Write unit tests for image-size-to-block-count validation. Must cover: (1) image exactly fits allocated blocks, (2) image is smaller than allocated blocks, (3) image exceeds allocated blocks by 1 byte, (4) image exceeds by a full block, (5) zero-size image, (6) preloader and FIP validated independently. The Valyrian limits: preloader max 0xfc*512=32256 bytes, FIP max 0x700*512=917504 bytes. This is a safety test - if validation fails, the system must refuse to erase or write flash.
close_reason: Merged into unbrk-g5s.16 (tests are part of implementation)

title: Integration tests: happy-path recovery against fixtures
description: Write end-to-end integration tests that run the complete recovery flow (and optionally the flash phase) against the fixture harness with mocked transport. The happy path test should: (1) replay a complete recovery transcript, (2) verify all state transitions occur in order, (3) verify correct events are emitted, (4) verify XMODEM transfers complete, (5) verify U-Boot prompt is detected. Also test the flash-phase happy path: loadaddr read, mmc erase, loadx+verify+write for preloader, loadx+verify+write for FIP, reset. The plan lists this as the M1 exit criterion: 'happy-path end-to-end recovery works against simulated fixtures'.

title: Integration tests: error and edge-case scenarios
description: Write integration tests for all documented error scenarios from the plan: (1) missing first prompt (timeout), (2) missing second prompt (timeout), (3) XMODEM cancel/failure, (4) sender reports failed final EOT but console advances (the quirk), (5) noisy console output between stages, (6) long DRAM/boot chatter between recovery stages, (7) ANSI-heavy boot-menu output before AN7581> prompt, (8) repeated C readiness mixed with non-printable control bytes, (9) mmc erase failure, (10) image larger than configured flash allocation, (11) filesize mismatch after loadx, (12) mmc write failure, (13) missing reset evidence after reset, (14) partial prompt fragmentation across reads. Each test should verify the correct error variant is returned with useful diagnostic context.

title: Mock/fake transport for testing
description: Implement a test-only transport that replays scripted byte sequences. This is the foundation for all unit and integration tests that run without hardware. Design: a MockTransport that takes a script of (direction, bytes, optional_delay) tuples. On read(), it returns the next scripted RX bytes. On write(), it asserts the TX bytes match expectations. Must support: (1) replaying captured UART transcripts, (2) simulating timing (prompt delays, XMODEM CRC readiness delays), (3) injecting errors (timeout, unexpected bytes), (4) partial reads (prompt text split across multiple read calls to test fragmentation handling). The Python helper's SerialConsole._buffer pattern shows the kind of buffering we need to test against.

title: Transcript capture (raw byte logging)
description: Implement raw-byte transcript capture that records every byte read from and written to the serial port. This serves two purposes: (1) debugging failed recoveries by examining the exact byte stream, (2) generating test fixtures from real hardware sessions. The plan requires --transcript-file <path> for raw UART bytes and --log-file <path> for structured logs. The transcript layer should wrap any Transport impl and tee all I/O to a file. Must preserve exact byte sequences including control characters, XMODEM framing bytes, and ANSI escapes. The Python helper does this via _record_rx() which extends a buffer and optionally writes to transcript file. On recovery failure, the plan says to auto-save a transcript.

title: Target profile types + Valyrian profile
description: Define the TargetProfile type and the concrete Valyrian instance. TargetProfile captures everything board-specific: prompt patterns (regex), serial defaults (baud, framing), flash layout (MMC block size, erase range, preloader start/count, FIP start/count), stage ordering, U-Boot prompt pattern. Use strong types: BlockOffset(u32), BlockCount(u32), MmcBlockSize(u32). The Valyrian profile: baud=115200, 8N1, no flow control, initial prompt='Press x', second prompt='Press x to load BL31 \+ U-Boot FIP', U-Boot prompt='AN7581>', erase range=0..0x800, preloader write=0x4/0xfc, FIP write=0x100/0x700. Critical: the second prompt CONTAINS the text of the first, so matching must be state-scoped. For v1, keep this as a simple struct - do NOT over-engineer into a trait hierarchy. The plan explicitly says generalizing to other boards is a non-goal.

comments.author: ynezz
comments.text: The target profile type system is designed for future extensibility to other Airoha boards, but v1 should NOT over-engineer this. Only Valyrian exists now. The profile should be a simple struct with all fields filled in, not a trait hierarchy. The plan says: 'Generalizing the Valyrian flash layout into a board-agnostic abstraction before the initial target works well' is a non-goal. But using strong types (BlockOffset, BlockCount) now prevents the mistake of adding a second board later and confusing offset/count semantics.

title: Valyrian target profile (concrete instance)
description: Create the concrete Valyrian target profile with all observed parameters: baud=115200, 8N1, no flow control, initial prompt='Press x', second prompt='Press x to load BL31 \+ U-Boot FIP', U-Boot prompt='AN7581>', MMC block size=512, erase range=0..0x800, preloader write=0x4/0xfc, FIP write=0x100/0x700. These values come directly from docs/valyrian-uart-recovery-protocol.md and tools/valyrian_uart_recovery.py. Critical detail: the second prompt CONTAINS the text of the first prompt ('Press x'), so matching must be state-scoped - the first-stage matcher must not match the longer second prompt, and the second prompt must only be sought after stage 1 completes. The Python helper handles this via start marks.
close_reason: Merged into unbrk-g5s.5 (target profile types now includes Valyrian instance)

comments.author: ynezz
comments.text: RISK: Prompt text may differ across bootloader revisions. The documented prompts ('Press x' and 'Press x to load BL31 + U-Boot FIP') come from a single observed session. Keep prompt regexes conservative and configurable (--uboot-prompt already planned). The plan says: 'use the existing log to bootstrap tests, then gather additional captures and keep prompt regexes conservative until variation is understood.' Mitigation: pattern-based matching + fresh transcript capture in M3.

title: Prompt parser with stage-local matching
description: Implement prompt detection that matches regex patterns against accumulated console output, with tests proving the stage-local matching invariant. Uses a cursor-based byte buffer: after each successful match, the cursor advances past the matched region so stale text cannot re-trigger a transition. Critical requirements: (1) state-scoped matching - each wait consumes only bytes after the preceding transition, (2) the first-stage matcher must NOT match the longer second prompt ('Press x' vs 'Press x to load BL31 + U-Boot FIP'), (3) tolerate line ending variations (CR, LF, CRLF), timing jitter, and partial prompt fragmentation across reads. Include unit tests covering: first/second prompt detection, prompt split across multiple reads, cursor advancement, timeout when prompt never arrives, and the critical stage-local invariant (stage 1 pattern does not consume stage 2 text).

comments.author: ynezz
comments.text: The prompt parser's cursor mechanism is the foundation of correctness. The Python helper uses mark()/text_since(start) - a simple integer cursor into a growing byte buffer. In Rust, we can use the same pattern with a Vec<u8> buffer and a usize cursor. After each successful match, the cursor advances past the matched region. This ensures: (1) stale text can't re-trigger a state transition, (2) the first 'Press x' match can't consume the longer second prompt, (3) XMODEM C readiness detection scans only fresh bytes. The buffer grows unboundedly during a session - acceptable for recovery sessions that last minutes, not hours.

title: Recovery state machine with EOT quirk tolerance
description: Implement the recovery state machine with 9 states: WaitForInitialPrompt, SendXForPreloader, WaitForXmodemCrcPreloader, SendPreloader, WaitForFipPrompt, SendXForFip, WaitForXmodemCrcFip, SendFip, WaitForUbootPrompt. Each transition emits a structured event and advances the byte-buffer cursor. Includes EOT quirk tolerance: if the XMODEM sender reports failure (no clean final ACK), check whether the expected next prompt already appeared in the console output. If yes, log a warning and continue (forward progress). If neither prompt nor ACK arrives within timeout, then fail. This quirk is well-documented: the Valyrian board can advance to the next prompt before ACKing the final XMODEM EOT. The state machine is transport-agnostic (works with mock or real serial). Each state has configurable timeout.

comments.author: ynezz
comments.text: The state machine is the architectural centerpiece. Consider using an enum-based design where each state variant carries its own context (expected pattern, timeout, cursor position). The transition function takes a state + input -> (next_state, events). This makes the state machine: (1) exhaustively matchable by the compiler (no missed states), (2) easy to serialize/log for debugging, (3) easy to test by driving transitions manually. Reference: the Python helper's reach_ram_uboot() function shows the linear happy-path flow, but a proper state machine can also handle re-entry (--resume-from-uboot) and error recovery more cleanly.

title: Stage-local matching (prevent cross-stage prompt confusion)
description: Implement and test the critical invariant that the first-stage prompt matcher cannot match the second-stage prompt text, and vice versa. The Valyrian protocol has an ambiguity: 'Press x' (stage 1) is a substring of 'Press x to load BL31 + U-Boot FIP' (stage 2). The Python helper handles this by using position marks - stage 2 prompt is only searched in bytes received AFTER stage 1 completes. Tests must cover: (1) stage 1 prompt arrives, stage 2 text not yet in buffer, (2) both prompts in buffer but cursor positioned after stage 1, (3) stage 2 prompt split across reads, (4) noise between stages that contains partial 'Press x' text. This is a known correctness hazard flagged in the plan - get it right with thorough test coverage before building on it.
close_reason: Merged into unbrk-g5s.7 (stage-local matching is a property of the prompt parser, not separate code)

title: Milestone 0: Repo Bootstrap
description: Bootstrap the Cargo workspace, toolchain config, repo policy files, CI skeleton, and common dev tooling. Exit criteria: clean CI on Linux/macOS/Windows with lint/test/release config checked in.

comments.author: ynezz
comments.text: This milestone gates all subsequent work. The Cargo workspace structure (unbrk-core + unbrk-cli + xtask) follows the plan's architecture: protocol logic in the library crate, rendering/IO in the CLI binary, automation in xtask. The workspace-level lint and dependency inheritance ensures consistency as the project grows. Clean CI on all three platforms (Linux/macOS/Windows) is the exit criterion - this proves the toolchain, workspace config, and quality gates are all functional before we start writing protocol code.

title: Cargo workspace + toolchain + build configuration
description: Create the root Cargo.toml with workspace configuration, rust-toolchain.toml, workspace-level lint configuration, and release profile. Specifically: (1) Root Cargo.toml defining workspace members (crates/unbrk-core, crates/unbrk-cli, xtask), [workspace.dependencies] for pinned dependency versions, [workspace.lints.rust] with unsafe_code='forbid', [workspace.lints.clippy] with pedantic+nursery enabled. (2) [profile.release] optimized for binary size: opt-level='z', lto=true, codegen-units=1, panic='abort', strip=true. Also [profile.dev.package.'*'] with opt-level=2 for faster dev iteration. (3) rust-toolchain.toml pinning a specific nightly date (edition 2024 requires nightly) with rustfmt and clippy components. This is the foundational commit that every other task builds on.
close_reason: Completed workspace/toolchain bootstrap

title: CI skeleton (GitHub Actions ci.yml)
description: Create .github/workflows/ci.yml triggered on PRs and pushes to main. Jobs: fmt check, clippy, nextest, doc tests, cargo deny check, typos. Build matrix for Linux/macOS/Windows. Pin all actions by full SHA for supply-chain security. Use least-privilege permissions (contents: read). Enable workflow concurrency cancellation. The CI must pass cleanly on all three platforms - that is the M0 exit criterion.
close_reason: Completed CI skeleton

title: CHANGELOG.md + CONTRIBUTING.md
description: Create initial CHANGELOG.md (Keep-a-Changelog format, empty Unreleased section). Create CONTRIBUTING.md documenting: local dev workflow (just fmt/lint/test/ci), Conventional Commits requirement, PR conventions, how to add fixtures, how to run integration tests. CONTRIBUTING.md should reference the justfile targets. These files are part of the M0 deliverables and set expectations for contributors and agents.
close_reason: Completed changelog and contributing docs

title: rust-toolchain.toml with stable channel + edition 2024
description: Create rust-toolchain.toml pinning the stable Rust channel. Rust edition 2024 has been stable since Rust 1.85.0 (Feb 2025), so nightly is NOT required. Use channel = "stable" (or a specific stable version like "1.94.0" for reproducibility). Include the rustfmt and clippy components. Set rust-version (MSRV) in Cargo.toml to 1.85 or later.
close_reason: Merged into unbrk-ije.1 (workspace + toolchain + build config)

title: unbrk-core crate scaffold
description: Create crates/unbrk-core/ with Cargo.toml and src/lib.rs. This is the library crate that will own all protocol logic: serial transport, prompt detection, state machine, XMODEM, U-Boot commands, flash plan, event model, and error taxonomy. Must inherit workspace lints. Must use 'lints.workspace = true' pattern. Keep lib.rs minimal with module declarations only. The core crate is the heart of the project - all protocol intelligence lives here, making it testable without the CLI layer.
close_reason: Completed core crate scaffold

title: unbrk-cli crate scaffold
description: Create crates/unbrk-cli/ with Cargo.toml and src/main.rs. This is the binary crate that depends on unbrk-core. It owns argument parsing (clap), TTY detection, progress rendering (indicatif), JSON event output, exit-code mapping, and console handoff. Must inherit workspace lints. main.rs should be minimal - just parse args and delegate to library functions. The CLI is a thin rendering/IO layer over the core library.
close_reason: Completed CLI crate scaffold

title: xtask crate scaffold
description: Create xtask/ with Cargo.toml and src/main.rs following the cargo-xtask pattern. Will own: shell completion generation, manpage generation, release sanity checks, and optional fixture generation from captured recovery transcripts. For now just scaffold the crate with a placeholder main that prints available commands. The xtask pattern avoids Makefile complexity and keeps all build automation in Rust.
close_reason: Completed xtask crate scaffold

title: Workspace lint configuration
description: Configure comprehensive workspace-level lints in root Cargo.toml under [workspace.lints.clippy] and [workspace.lints.rust]. Enable clippy::pedantic and clippy::nursery lint groups. Forbid unsafe_code. Deny warnings in CI. Each member crate uses 'lints.workspace = true' to inherit. This is critical because the plan specifies 'cargo clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings' as a quality gate. Pedantic + nursery catches subtle bugs and enforces idiomatic Rust patterns.
close_reason: Merged into unbrk-ije.1

title: Release profile optimized for binary size
description: Configure [profile.release] in root Cargo.toml: opt-level='z' (optimize for size), lto=true, codegen-units=1, panic='abort', strip=true. This is a CLI binary distributed to end users, so binary size matters more than compile speed. Also add [profile.dev.package.'*'] with opt-level=2 so dependencies compile with optimization even in dev builds, improving iteration speed on protocol testing.
close_reason: Merged into unbrk-ije.1

title: Repo policy files (.editorconfig, deny.toml)
description: Create .editorconfig for consistent formatting across editors (indent_style=space, indent_size=4 for Rust, charset=utf-8, trim_trailing_whitespace, insert_final_newline). Create deny.toml for cargo-deny: configure advisories (deny unmaintained, deny yanked), license policy (allow MIT/Apache-2.0/BSD-*, deny copyleft), and bans section. These policy files enforce supply-chain hygiene from day one.
close_reason: Completed repo policy files

title: justfile with common dev targets
description: Create justfile with targets for every common task: 'just fmt', 'just lint' (clippy --workspace --all-targets -- -D warnings), 'just test' (nextest run --workspace), 'just doc-test' (test --doc --workspace), 'just deny' (cargo deny check), 'just ci' (runs all quality gates in sequence), 'just dist' (placeholder for release builds). The plan explicitly calls for just targets for every common task to make the repo agent-friendly. Agents and humans should use the same commands.
close_reason: Completed justfile bootstrap

title: Milestone 4: Release Hardening
description: Set up release-plz automation, cargo-dist artifacts, checksums, attestations, Dependabot, and cargo-deny. Exit criteria: tagged release produces installable artifacts for all primary platforms.

title: release-plz setup (versioning + changelog automation)
description: Configure release-plz for semantic versioning, release PRs, and changelog automation. Create .github/workflows/release-pr.yml that: opens/updates release PRs, bumps versions according to conventional commits, updates CHANGELOG.md, creates Git tags and GitHub Releases after merge. Use Conventional Commits format. Start at 0.x until recovery flow and CLI contract settle. Minor bumps for features, patch bumps for fixes. The plan says: 'Major 1.0 only after the recovery flow is proven stable and the machine interface is intentionally versioned.'

title: cargo-dist setup (cross-platform release artifacts)
description: Configure cargo-dist to build release artifacts. Create .github/workflows/dist.yml. Target platforms: x86_64-unknown-linux-gnu, x86_64-pc-windows-msvc, x86_64-apple-darwin, aarch64-apple-darwin. Optional later: aarch64-unknown-linux-gnu, x86_64-unknown-linux-musl. Artifacts: archives (tar.gz for Linux/macOS, zip for Windows), SHA-256 checksums, optional shell/PowerShell installer scripts. Integrate with GitHub Releases so each tagged version gets downloadable binaries.

title: Checksums and provenance attestations
description: Generate SHA-256 checksums for all release artifacts and publish alongside binaries. Set up GitHub artifact attestations using actions/attest-build-provenance for supply-chain transparency. This lets users verify: (1) integrity via checksums, (2) provenance via attestations (artifact was built by this repo's CI, not tampered). The plan says: 'Generate attestations for release artifacts' as part of the security and supply chain requirements.

title: First public prerelease (0.1.0)
description: Cut the first public prerelease after hardware validation succeeds. Tag as v0.1.0. Publish release artifacts to GitHub Releases. Optionally enable crates.io trusted publishing for cargo install - but only if the CLI contract has been declared ready. The plan says: 'cargo install is enabled only if the CLI contract has been declared ready for public installation.' This is the project's public debut - make sure README, CHANGELOG, and documentation are polished.

comments.author: ynezz
comments.text: The 0.1.0 prerelease is the project's first public artifact. Before tagging: (1) README must have a human-driven e2e recovery example, a machine-driven --json example, and a stop-at-uboot example, (2) CHANGELOG must describe what works and what doesn't, (3) exit codes must be documented, (4) JSON event schema must be documented or at least versioned. Versioning policy: 0.x until the recovery flow and CLI contract settle. The plan explicitly defers crates.io publishing until the CLI contract is declared ready.

title: Dependabot configuration
description: Create .github/dependabot.yml to enable automated dependency updates for both GitHub Actions (weekly) and Cargo dependencies (weekly). The plan requires this as part of supply-chain security. Pin Actions by full SHA in workflows, then rely on Dependabot to propose SHA bumps when new versions release. For Cargo deps, Dependabot PRs go through the full CI quality gate before merge.

title: cargo-deny advisory and license policy
description: Refine deny.toml with production-ready advisory and license policies. Advisory section: deny unmaintained crates, deny yanked crates, configure severity thresholds. License section: allow MIT/Apache-2.0/BSD variants, deny copyleft (GPL/LGPL/AGPL). Bans section: deny specific crates if needed. This builds on the initial deny.toml from M0 but hardens it for release readiness. Run cargo deny check as part of CI and pre-release checks.
